planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Gypsy's earlier maturity (60–70 days) compared to bell peppers (70–80 days) gives zone 9 gardeners an earlier start on sweet pepper harvest.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Gypsy is an All-America Selections winner (1981) — a sweet, elongated, tapering pepper that produces medium-sized (4 inches long, 2.5 inches wide), semi-thick-walled fruits that ripen through a distinctive color progression from pale creamy-yellow, through orange, to bright red, with excellent flavor at every stage. The early maturity (60–70 days) gives Gypsy a head start on the sweet pepper season compared to larger bell types, and the disease resistance package includes tobacco mosaic virus tolerance — a significant advantage. Gypsy is a semi-sweet, elongated type rather than a classic blocky bell — closer to an Italian frying pepper shape but with thicker walls and a sweeter, less pungent flavor than traditional frying types. The elongated fruits are outstanding for fresh eating, stuffing (the tapering shape holds stuffings differently than blocks), grilling, and roasting. The color progression from pale yellow through orange to red creates an attractive multi-colored harvest display when fruits at different maturity stages are present on the plant simultaneously. Plants are productive, compact, and well-suited to both spring and fall production in zone 9. An outstanding early-maturing sweet pepper for home garden use.

common_name: Sweet Banana Pepper (Classic Long Yellow Sweet Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Sweet Banana peppers are excellent for pickling — harvest at the pale yellow to light orange stage for maximum crispness in pickled applications.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Sweet Banana is one of the most popular sweet pepper varieties in American home gardens — a long (6–8 inch), tapering, banana-shaped pepper with thin to medium walls and very mild, sweet flavor with absolutely no heat at any stage of maturity. The fruits ripen from pale creamy-yellow through orange to red, with the best flavor for fresh eating at the orange to red stage and the best texture for pickling at the pale yellow stage. Sweet banana peppers are the standard pepper for the famous deli-style pickled banana peppers — the crunchy, mildly tangy, bright-yellow pickled pepper slices ubiquitous on Italian subs, pizza, and in Greek salads. Home-pickled banana peppers from fresh-harvested yellow fruits are far superior in flavor and crunch to commercially processed versions. For fresh use, allow to ripen fully red — the red stage develops sweeter, richer flavor approaching the sweetness of roasted red pepper. The productive, upright, sturdy plants are easily distinguished by the numerous hanging yellow peppers at varying stages, creating a colorful harvest display. In zone 9, sweet banana peppers are among the most productive warm-season vegetables — the long growing season allows multiple picks per plant from spring through late fall.

common_name: Purple Beauty Bell Pepper (Purple-to-Red Blocky Bell Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Purple Beauty's unusual purple color is most vivid when fruits first reach harvest size — color fades toward red as they fully mature.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Purple Beauty is the classic purple bell pepper — producing blocky, four-lobed, standard bell-pepper-shaped fruits that mature to a distinctive purple to dark purple-violet color rather than the usual green-to-red sequence. The unusual purple color at harvest stage creates striking visual interest in garden beds and in fresh salads where the vivid purple contrasts dramatically with red tomatoes, orange carrots, and yellow peppers. The purple color fades to red as the fruit fully matures; for maximum visual impact, harvest at the purple stage when the anthocyanin pigments are fully developed. As an open-pollinated variety, seeds can be saved true-to-type. The flavor at the purple stage is similar to green bell pepper — crisp and mildly sweet — while fully ripe red peppers are somewhat sweeter. Purple Beauty provides ornamental value in the vegetable garden even before harvest: the purple fruits hanging from the plants create a visually striking effect among the green foliage. The compact plant (18–30 inches) is well-suited to both in-ground and container growing. Anthocyanin content makes purple bell peppers nutritionally distinct from green or red types, with the same antioxidant compounds found in blueberries and blackberries.

common_name: Golden California Wonder Pepper (Gold-Ripening Open-Pollinated Bell Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Golden California Wonder is the gold counterpart to the standard red-ripening California Wonder — same fruit form and size, different final color.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Golden California Wonder is the gold-maturing selection from the California Wonder bell pepper family — producing the same blocky, four-lobed, large, thick-walled bell pepper fruit as standard California Wonder but ripening to a golden-yellow color rather than red. The fruits begin green and ripen to a warm, rich golden-yellow at full maturity — a color stage with sweeter, more mellow flavor than the green stage and a different flavor profile from fully red bell peppers. Golden bell peppers are noticeably sweeter and lower in Scoville units than green bell peppers — the color change from green to gold/yellow reflects the conversion of chlorophyll to carotenoids and the simultaneous conversion of starches to sugars. As an open-pollinated variety, Golden California Wonder seeds can be saved reliably from season to season. The golden fruits create visual contrast in mixed salads, stir-fries, and grilled vegetable platters where the gold color pairs beautifully with red and orange companions. The same thick walls and blocky form as California Wonder make Golden California Wonder outstanding for stuffed peppers. In zone 9, plant in spring (February–March) for a spring crop and in August for a fall crop.

planting.note: Plant corms 4 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost date; in zone 9, plant corms in February–March for summer bloom; in zone 9 the corms may overwinter in the ground if protected with 3–4 inches of mulch or in a warm, sheltered location.

directions: Plant corms 4 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost date; Peacock Orchid (also known as Acidanthera or Abyssinian gladiolus) is one of the most fragrant summer bulbs available for the warm-season garden — producing tall, slender stems (3–4 feet) bearing 6–8 staggered, pure-white, star-shaped flowers with a prominent, deep maroon-purple blotch at the center of each flower, creating a dramatic color contrast. The intense, sweet, orchid-like fragrance that distinguishes Peacock Orchid from all other gladiolus relatives is its most celebrated quality: a single stem in a vase perfumes an entire room, and a planting of 20–30 corms fills the garden with fragrance on warm evenings. The flowers open successively along the spike over 2–3 weeks, extending the display season. The combination of the white flower with dark center, tall elegant stem, and exceptional fragrance makes Peacock Orchid outstanding for cutting gardens. The corms are somewhat tender and must be lifted and stored in zone 6 and colder; in zone 9, they often overwinter successfully in well-drained soil with mulch protection and naturalize to form expanding clumps over several years. Plant in succession every 2–3 weeks from March through May for continuous summer bloom. The fragrant flowers open in the afternoon and evening, making Peacock Orchid particularly effective near patios and outdoor seating areas.
